note: Reader-agent judgments with VERBATIM basis quotes (workflow ds9-eligibility-quotes). Each basis[].quote is copied from a Memory Alpha page and is pinned + verified + affirmative-checked by ds9-eligibility-adjudicate.mjs. Silence => empty basis => review.

basis.quote: Her first day on set started at 4:30am for makeup which consisted of a wig and some airbrushing.
basis.establishes: The affirmative makeup description for Cretak (as played by Adrienne Barbeau) is a wig and airbrushing rather than heavy prosthetics; Romulan is a designed alien species but this quote reads light, so eligibility is borderline.

basis.quote: Michael Westmore designed the makeup for Cretak.
basis.establishes: Confirms a designed Romulan makeup was applied to the character.

basis.quote: Shane was played by actor Alan Ladd.
basis.establishes: Shane is a Western film character played by human actor Alan Ladd.

basis.quote: The brief clips from Shane that appeared in this episode featured shots of Alan Ladd as Shane.
basis.establishes: The audience sees actual film footage of Alan Ladd as himself, no designed face.

basis.quote: Julian Bashir was played by Alexander Siddig.
basis.establishes: Human main character portrayed by Alexander Siddig, seen bare-faced throughout the series.

basis.quote: Bashir was played by Alexander Siddig.
basis.establishes: Terran (human-appearance) mirror counterpart played by the same bare-faced actor, Alexander Siddig, distinguished only by a scruffy beard.

basis.quote: Julius Eaton was played by Alexander Siddig.
basis.establishes: A Human 1950s science-fiction writer in Sisko's vision, played by Alexander Siddig without alien makeup.

basis.quote: At the time of the episode's filming, the actor was married to Nana Visitor, who played his wife Kay.
basis.establishes: Confirms the human 1953 casting where the DS9 actors appear as themselves.

basis.quote: Torias was played by Alexander Siddig during Jadzia's zhian'tara.
basis.establishes: On screen Torias is embodied by Alexander Siddig (as Bashir's body), a bare-faced human actor with only Trill spot makeup.

basis.quote: During Jadzia Dax' zhian'tara in 2371, Julian Bashir embodied Torias' memories and personality.
basis.establishes: The character is seen as Bashir/Siddig himself, not under a designed face.

basis.quote: Nina was played by Andrea Robinson.
basis.establishes: A Human character in the 1962-Las Vegas Bashir 62 holoprogram played by Andrea Robinson; identifies the performer but the page contains no affirmative statement about makeup, so bare-faced appearance cannot be affirmatively confirmed from prose.

basis.quote: Garak was played by actor Andrew Robinson.
basis.establishes: Identifies the performer under the Cardassian makeup.

basis.quote: The makeup featured not only shoulders and the forehead piece but also a chin piece and a nose appliance.
basis.establishes: Documents the full Cardassian prosthetic makeup (forehead, chin, nose appliances) that the actor vanishes beneath.

basis.quote: At about the start of DS9's series run, the makeup took around four hours.
basis.establishes: The multi-hour Cardassian prosthetic application confirms a heavy designed face.

basis.quote: Like his counterpart, Garak was portrayed by Andrew Robinson.
basis.establishes: Mirror Garak is a Cardassian played by Andrew Robinson, same full prosthetic makeup as the prime counterpart.

basis.quote: The makeup featured not only shoulders and the forehead piece but also a chin piece and a nose appliance.
basis.establishes: Documents the full Cardassian prosthetic makeup the actor disappears under.

basis.quote: Nadia Larkin was played by Annette Helde.
basis.establishes: A Human Starfleet officer played by Annette Helde; identifies the performer but the page gives no affirmative makeup/bare-faced statement, so it cannot be positively confirmed from prose.

basis.quote: Audrid was played by Armin Shimerman during Jadzia Dax's zhian'tara.
basis.establishes: On screen Audrid is embodied by Armin Shimerman appearing as Quark, i.e. in full Ferengi prosthetics.

basis.quote: During Jadzia Dax's zhian'tara in 2371, Quark embodied Audrid's memories and personality.
basis.establishes: The audience sees Quark (a Ferengi under heavy prosthetics), not the performer's own face.

basis.quote: It was bizarre to be bare-faced on a Star Trek show. I never had been before.
basis.establishes: Armin Shimerman played Rossoff bare-faced, out of his usual Ferengi makeup, so the audience sees the performer as himself.

basis.quote: The lack of Ferengi make-up allowed Shimerman three extra hours of sleep every day than he usually was permitted.
basis.establishes: The human Rossoff role required no Ferengi prosthetics.

basis.quote: Armin Shimerman initially appeared as Quark in Emissary with the prosthetic nose designed for Max Grodénchik's Rom, as his nose was still being created.
basis.establishes: Quark is played under prosthetic Ferengi facial appliances.

basis.quote: The prosthetic nose designed specially for Shimerman first appeared in A Man Alone.
basis.establishes: A dedicated prosthetic was built for Shimerman's Quark face.

basis.quote: It consisted of two prosthetic appliances. The first was the helmet-like headpiece, which stretched from the wearer's eyebrows, over the person's head, to the back of his or her neck.
basis.establishes: Nog (a Ferengi, played by Aron Eisenberg) is hidden under full prosthetic Ferengi head appliances covering the whole head and face.

basis.quote: The Ferengi makeup was generally accepted as one of the hardest Star Trek prosthetic schemes to endure.
basis.establishes: The Ferengi look is a heavy full prosthetic makeup scheme.

basis.quote: When Avery Brooks' agent first rang him to tell him that there was a role available in a new Star Trek show, Brooks laughed, because he instinctively felt he was going to be offered a role requiring heavy prosthetics, which he wasn't interested in doing.
basis.establishes: Sisko is not a heavy-prosthetics role.

basis.quote: After finding out the role was that of a Human, Brooks was still unconvinced
basis.establishes: Sisko is a Human role, played by Avery Brooks as himself.

basis.quote: Hippocrates Noah was played by Avery Brooks, who also played his normal role as Captain Sisko in this episode.
basis.establishes: Noah is Avery Brooks playing a human holo-villain in his normal (non-prosthetic) form, seen as himself.

basis.quote: The executives insisted the make-up be changed via removal of the forehead markings; having spent a long time seeking a beautiful actress for the role of Dax, Paramount wanted to avoid Farrell being defaced by prosthetics.
basis.establishes: The DS9 Trill makeup (which Joran, a DS9 Trill host, wears) deliberately dropped forehead prosthetics so the performer is not hidden.

basis.quote: It's not much different than freckles or leopard spots or zebra stripes,
basis.establishes: The Trill look is only light spot makeup, leaving the performer visible as themselves.

basis.quote: Together with her on-screen sister and fellow Canadian Gwynyth Walsh, March was a frequent guest at Star Trek-related conventions such as Trek Expo 2008 and the Creation Las Vegas Star Trek Conventions 2010 and 2011, also appearing in Klingon makeup and costume.
basis.establishes: Barbara March, who played the Klingon Lursa, appeared in Klingon makeup, i.e. under the species' prosthetic makeup.

basis.quote: He based the spine on the length of Dorn's actual spine, but made each single Klingon vertebra the same size as two Human vertebrae.
basis.establishes: The Klingon appearance is a designed prosthetic makeup construction (Westmore-built).

basis.quote: The actor had wanted to appear on DS9 ever since the show's first season, and was offered many alien roles, but didn't want to wear extensive prosthetics.
basis.establishes: Bill Mumy played the Human crewman Kellin bare-faced, specifically refusing extensive prosthetics, so the audience sees the performer as himself.

basis.quote: Lieutenant Synon was a female Klingon warrior.
basis.establishes: Synon is a Klingon, a species rendered with prosthetic makeup appliances.

basis.quote: For scenes from "Ethics" featuring actor Michael Dorn as Worf, Michael Westmore designed Klingon feet and a Klingon spine.
basis.establishes: Klingons are realized through designed prosthetic makeup appliances (Michael Westmore's Klingon makeup work).

basis.quote: The prosthetics for Yint's species somewhat resemble the Tak Tak face, with the top and back of a Rotciv covering the rest of the head.
basis.establishes: Yint wore full-head prosthetics covering the rest of the head, hiding the performer under a designed face.

basis.quote: Members of his species bore ridges along the top of their heads, and in the middle of their faces.
basis.establishes: Yint's face was covered with sculpted ridges (a designed alien face).

basis.quote: Krit's species were a green-skinned humanoid race from the Alpha or Beta Quadrant.
basis.establishes: Krit's whole face/skin was colored green via makeup, a designed face rather than the performer's own appearance.

basis.quote: This species was rather similar to Grathon Tolar's species, with the biggest difference (aside from the color) being the lack of the ridge from the lip to below the chin.
basis.establishes: The species' look is a sculpted makeup design (color plus facial ridges) comparable to another prosthetic alien species.

basis.quote: Starrett was played by actor Brandon De Wilde.
basis.establishes: Joey Starrett is a Human film character played by a Human actor with no designed-face element.

basis.quote: The brief clips from Shane that appeared in this episode featured shots of Brandon De Wilde as Joey Starrett.
basis.establishes: The character appears only via archival film footage of the actor as himself (bare-faced Human).

basis.quote: Toman'torax was a Jem'Hadar soldier in service of the Dominion.
basis.establishes: Toman'torax is a Jem'Hadar, a species realized with full-facial prosthetic masks.

basis.quote: The full-facial prosthetic masks used for the Jem'Hadar involved a lot of latex.
basis.establishes: Jem'Hadar performers were hidden under full-facial latex prosthetic masks.

basis.quote: Talura's species were a humanoid species from the Alpha or Beta Quadrant that had slightly raised cranial ridges.
basis.establishes: Larell's species has only slightly raised cranial ridges, a light appliance that leaves the performer's face visible.

basis.quote: According to the scripts for these appearances, Talura's alien features were likened to those of the Boslic Captain, while the others were simply described as a "beautiful Alien woman."
basis.establishes: Larell (one of "the others") was scripted simply as a beautiful woman, i.e. the performer is seen largely as herself.

basis.quote: Joseph Sisko was played by Brock Peters.
basis.establishes: A Human contemporary character (New Orleans chef) played by Human actor Brock Peters, but the page gives no affirmative makeup/bare-faced production note.

basis.quote: Sarish Rez was a Bajoran and First Minister Shakaar Edon's adjutant.
basis.establishes: Sarish is a Bajoran, whose only makeup is a small nose appliance.

basis.quote: Because each of the actors cast as Bajorans had to have their nosepiece applied each day, the DS9 makeup department had to have enough makeup artists to put on all the prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: Bajoran makeup is limited to a nosepiece, a light appliance that leaves the performer's face visible as themselves.

basis.quote: just before my first shot, my first day in all this makeup!
basis.establishes: Casey Biggs describes being in extensive Cardassian prosthetic makeup to play Damar, so the performer is hidden under a designed alien face.

basis.quote: although she never had Bajoran nose ridges in the series.
basis.establishes: The character wore no prosthetic nose ridges, so the human actress (Cathy DeBuono) appeared essentially as herself with no designed alien face.

basis.quote: didn't recognize her as having played Ishka, as the actress wasn't wearing her Ferengi makeup.
basis.establishes: The performer was unrecognizable out of makeup, confirming she vanished under the Ferengi prosthetic makeup when in character.

basis.quote: with drooping [[lobe]]s and large jowls.
basis.establishes: Describes the full Ferengi head-piece prosthetic (drooping lobes, large jowls) worn to portray the character.

basis.quote: The full-facial prosthetic masks used for the Jem'Hadar involved a lot of latex.
basis.establishes: Jem'Hadar (Lamat'Ukan's species) were portrayed with full-facial latex prosthetic masks, so the performer vanishes under a designed creature face.

basis.quote: The Vorta makeup took approximately two and a half hours to be applied.
basis.establishes: Keevan's species, the Vorta, were portrayed with lengthy prosthetic makeup (prosthetic ears, contacts, altered skin).

basis.quote: I had no idea what a Vorta looked like until the makeup was done at 6:30 in the morning, when I looked in the mirror
basis.establishes: The Vorta look is a full makeup transformation that renders the performer's own appearance unrecognizable.

basis.quote: The full-facial prosthetic masks used for the Jem'Hadar involved a lot of latex.
basis.establishes: Omet'iklan is a Jem'Hadar, a species whose members wore full-facial latex prosthetic masks, hiding the performer under a designed face.

basis.quote: The makeup for this species strongly resembles that of the make-up worn by the Delta Quadrant species Vhnori, as depicted in VOY: "Emanations".
basis.establishes: Fuchida is a member of an alien species defined by facial makeup resembling the Vhnori prosthetic appliances, so the performer appears under a designed alien face.

basis.quote: Falcon wore an eyepatch with the image of a falcon's head over his left eye.
basis.establishes: Falcon's only disguise element is an eyepatch; the character has a Human appearance and the performer's face is otherwise seen as themselves.

basis.quote: Tobin Dax was played by Colm Meaney during Jadzia Dax's zhian'tara in the episode "Facets".
basis.establishes: Tobin appears only via the zhian'tara ritual, portrayed using regular cast member Colm Meaney.

basis.quote: During Jadzia Dax's zhian'tara in 2371, Miles O'Brien embodied Tobin's memories and personality.
basis.establishes: On screen Tobin is seen as Miles O'Brien (Colm Meaney) himself embodying the personality, not under Trill prosthetics.

basis.quote: Despite the role being recast, Batten and Middendorf wore the same foam latex facial prosthetics as each other.
basis.establishes: The performers playing Ziyal wore foam latex facial prosthetics, a designed Cardassian/Bajoran face.

basis.quote: Well, I loved that the makeup allowed me to disappear, but the makeup is why I left the show.
basis.establishes: Actress Melanie Smith states the Cardassian makeup made her disappear into the role, confirming the performer is hidden under the designed face.

basis.quote: Krit's species were a green-skinned humanoid race from the Alpha or Beta Quadrant.
basis.establishes: Nahsk's species has fully recolored (green) skin, i.e. the performer's natural face/skin is covered by a designed alien makeup.

basis.quote: This species was rather similar to Grathon Tolar's species, with the biggest difference (aside from the color) being the lack of the ridge from the lip to below the chin.
basis.establishes: The species' face is a designed makeup appliance (facial ridges/coloring), a background/production note describing the appliance design.

basis.quote: Broik was a Ferengi waiter at Quark's on Deep Space 9 between 2369 and 2375.
basis.establishes: Broik is a Ferengi, one of the full-prosthetic DS9 alien species.

basis.quote: It consisted of two prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: Ferengi makeup is a full head/face prosthetic scheme (helmet-like headpiece plus an appliance covering the nose).

basis.quote: The Ferengi makeup was generally accepted as one of the hardest Star Trek prosthetic schemes to endure.
basis.establishes: Ferengi is heavy prosthetics; the performer vanishes under the designed face.

basis.quote: The Letant makeup is given a quick touch-up
basis.establishes: Letant wore facial makeup, described as a light touch-up rather than a full mask/prosthetic head.

basis.quote: Some Romulans had two brow ridges above the bridge of their nose, forming a V-shape on the forehead, while other Romulans lacked these ridges, making them outwardly indistinguishable from Vulcans.
basis.establishes: Romulan appearance is near-human (brow ridge plus pointed ears); the actor's face is largely visible, so the audience mostly sees the performer as themselves.

basis.quote: Basso was a Bajoran collaborator who worked for the Cardassians during the occupation of Bajor.
basis.establishes: Basso is Bajoran, a species whose makeup is limited to a nose appliance.

basis.quote: Visitor was relieved by the news that the Bajoran makeup was one of the most minor in the series.
basis.establishes: Bajoran makeup is explicitly one of the most minor in the series (a nose piece), so the performer is seen essentially as themselves.

basis.quote: Because each of the actors cast as Bajorans had to have their nosepiece applied each day, the DS9 makeup department had to have enough makeup artists to put on all the prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: The Bajoran appliance is only a nosepiece (light nasal appliance).

basis.quote: Lieutenant Onara was an operations division officer in the Bajoran Militia who served on Deep Space 9 during the 2370s.
basis.establishes: Onara is Bajoran, a species whose makeup is limited to a nose appliance.

basis.quote: Visitor was relieved by the news that the Bajoran makeup was one of the most minor in the series.
basis.establishes: Bajoran makeup is one of the most minor in the series (a nose piece), so the performer is seen essentially as themselves.

basis.quote: Because each of the actors cast as Bajorans had to have their nosepiece applied each day, the DS9 makeup department had to have enough makeup artists to put on all the prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: The Bajoran appliance is only a nosepiece (light nasal appliance).

basis.quote: Riley Aldrin Shepard was a human in the 24th century.
basis.establishes: Shepard is a Human character (Starfleet cadet), played bare-faced with no alien prosthetics.

basis.quote: Melissa was a holographic character in the Bashir 62 holoprogram.
basis.establishes: Melissa is a human-appearing 1960s Las Vegas showgirl hologram in Vic Fontaine's program; the actress appears bare-faced as herself.

basis.quote: Sarah Sisko was a female Human citizen of the 24th century Federation.
basis.establishes: Sarah Sisko is a Human character, played bare-faced with no alien prosthetics.

basis.quote: Admiral T'Lara was a 24th century Vulcan Starfleet flag officer.
basis.establishes: T'Lara is Vulcan, a species whose makeup is limited to ear tips and eyebrows.

basis.quote: For Star Trek Into Darkness, Vulcan ear prosthetics, in encapsulated silicone, were created by makeup artist David Snyder.
basis.establishes: Vulcan makeup is limited to ear prosthetics; the actor's face is otherwise fully visible.

basis.quote: Tilikia was a Boslic freighter captain.
basis.establishes: Tilikia is a Boslic, and the male Boslic prosthetics were used in his episode 'Sons of Mogh'.

basis.quote: Following her second appearance, the male Boslic was "officially" introduced in the fourth season episode "Sons of Mogh", however, their prosthetics did not resemble those of the aforementioned female, but rather were the same prosthetics worn by the unspecified alien Jaheel in the first season episode "Babel".
basis.establishes: The male Boslic in 'Sons of Mogh' (Tilikia's episode) wore facial prosthetics (ridged forehead, wrinkled nose, creased cheek bones), a designed face.

basis.quote: Because each of the actors cast as Bajorans had to have their nosepiece applied each day, the DS9 makeup department had to have enough makeup artists to put on all the prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: Shakaar is a Bajoran, and Bajoran makeup consisted only of a small nose piece, so the performer's face is seen as himself.

basis.quote: We've hired a pretty girl and I want to keep her that way. Think of something that we can take and make her look a little alien, and still get the idea she's from another planet, but she's still gorgeous.
basis.establishes: The Bajoran makeup was deliberately minimal so the actor remains recognizable/seen as themselves.

basis.quote: The effect of spreading lesions—as seen on the Teplan Epran when exposed to the EM fields of Bashir's medical equipment—were created digitally by Gary Hutzel using multilayer compositing, a relatively novel approach at the time.
basis.establishes: Epran's blight appearance was partly a digital lesion effect over an otherwise human-looking Teplan, not a full designed face.

basis.quote: The practical lesion effects were created by Michael Westmore's makeup effects team by sculpting veins in clay on a flat piece of plaster, then placing a rubber mold over the veins.
basis.establishes: The Teplan blight was applied lesion/vein makeup on the skin rather than a full-face prosthetic mask; borderline between light makeup and heavy prosthetics.

basis.quote: The practical lesion effects were created by Michael Westmore's makeup effects team by sculpting veins in clay on a flat piece of plaster, then placing a rubber mold over the veins.
basis.establishes: Ekoria is a Teplan; the blight was applied lesion/vein makeup over otherwise human-looking skin, not a full designed face; borderline.

basis.quote: It consisted of two prosthetic appliances. The first was the helmet-like headpiece, which stretched from the wearer's eyebrows, over the person's head, to the back of his or her neck.
basis.establishes: Frool is a Ferengi, and Ferengi makeup was two full prosthetic appliances including a helmet-like headpiece covering the whole head, hiding the performer under a designed face.

basis.quote: a young Human crewman with an easy, infectious smile.
basis.establishes: Muniz is a Human character described by his visible, smiling bare face, so the performer (F.J. Rio) is seen as himself with no alien prosthetics.

basis.quote: The camera was going to zoom down on my face when my mouth was wide open.
basis.establishes: Sarina is a Human character and the actress's own bare face was shot in close-up on camera, so the performer is seen as herself.

basis.quote: When Cardassians began to appear in Star Trek, the makeup for the species took three-and-a-half hours to apply.
basis.establishes: Dolak is a Cardassian, and Cardassian makeup was a multi-hour full prosthetic scheme, hiding the performer under a designed face.

basis.quote: The makeup featured not only shoulders and the forehead piece but also a chin piece and a nose appliance.
basis.establishes: Cardassian makeup comprised multiple facial prosthetic pieces (forehead, chin, nose), a full designed face.

basis.quote: The full-facial prosthetic masks used for the Jem'Hadar involved a lot of latex.
basis.establishes: Ixtana'Rax is a Jem'Hadar, and the Jem'Hadar were played wearing full-facial latex prosthetic masks, hiding the performer under a designed face.

basis.quote: The most distinctive feature of the modern Klingon was a sagittal crest, beginning on the forehead and often continuing over the skull.
basis.establishes: N'Garen is a Klingon; the modern Klingon's defining designed feature is the prosthetic cranial forehead crest covering the head.

basis.quote: For scenes from "Ethics" featuring actor Michael Dorn as Worf, Michael Westmore designed Klingon feet and a Klingon spine.
basis.establishes: Klingon appearance required prosthetic makeup appliances designed by the makeup supervisor, confirming Klingons are a full-prosthetic species.

basis.quote: Because each of the actors cast as Bajorans had to have their nosepiece applied each day, the DS9 makeup department had to have enough makeup artists to put on all the prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: Korena is a Bajoran, and Bajoran makeup was only a small nose piece, so the performer's face is seen as herself.

basis.quote: We've hired a pretty girl and I want to keep her that way. Think of something that we can take and make her look a little alien, and still get the idea she's from another planet, but she's still gorgeous.
basis.establishes: Bajoran makeup was deliberately minimal so the actor remains recognizable/seen as themselves.

basis.quote: an imposing Human Alien male
basis.establishes: The script describes Hain as a 'Human Alien' (Memory Alpha's term for an essentially human-looking alien), i.e. actor Gregory Itzin is seen bare-faced with no designed alien face.

basis.quote: For scenes from "Ethics" featuring actor Michael Dorn as Worf, Michael Westmore designed Klingon feet and a Klingon spine.
basis.establishes: Klingons are realized through Michael Westmore-designed prosthetic makeup appliances; the species (including the iconic ridged cranial crest) is a designed face, hiding the performer.

basis.quote: makeup artist Michael Westmore gave the Romulans V-shaped forehead ridges
basis.establishes: Romulan makeup consists of a forehead ridge appliance and pointed ear tips, but the performer's face remains substantially visible; this is borderline for the wall (Romulan is not among the parent's auto-eligible full-prosthetic species).

basis.quote: It consisted of two prosthetic appliances. The first was the helmet-like headpiece, which stretched from the wearer's eyebrows, over the person's head, to the back of his or her neck.
basis.establishes: Ferengi are realized with two large prosthetic appliances (headpiece plus ear-to-ear/nose piece) taking about three hours to apply, completely hiding the performer under a designed face.

basis.quote: Molly O'Brien was the Human daughter of Keiko and Miles O'Brien.
basis.establishes: Molly is a Human child played by a human child actor (Hana Hatae), seen bare-faced as herself with no designed alien face.

basis.quote: It consisted of two prosthetic appliances. The first was the helmet-like headpiece, which stretched from the wearer's eyebrows, over the person's head, to the back of his or her neck.
basis.establishes: Nilva is a Ferengi; the species is realized with two large prosthetic appliances (helmet-like headpiece plus nose/ear piece), hiding actor Henry Gibson under a designed face.

basis.quote: The hardest aspect of portraying Jaresh-Inyo was enduring the Grazerite makeup required for the role, although Sparber felt relaxed about having it put on and taken off.
basis.establishes: Actor Herschel Sparber wore extensive Grazerite makeup appliances for the role, a designed alien face that hides the performer.

basis.quote: Lauren was a genetically-engineered Human born during the 24th century.
basis.establishes: Lauren is a Human (genetically-engineered) character played by actress Hilary Shepard Turner seen as herself, with no designed alien face.

basis.quote: The Vorta makeup took approximately two and a half hours to be applied.
basis.establishes: The Vorta are realized through roughly two-and-a-half hours of prosthetic makeup (including manufactured Vorta ears, a wig and contacts), a designed face that hides the performer (Iggy Pop).

basis.quote: consisted of manufacturing Vorta ears for the character of Weyoun
basis.establishes: Vorta appearance requires manufactured prosthetic ears among other appliances, confirming a designed face rather than the performer's own.

basis.quote: Rita Tannenbaum was portrayed by regular background actress Ivy Borg who appeared throughout the seven year run of Star Trek: Deep Space Nine.
basis.establishes: Rita is a Human Starfleet officer played bare-faced by recurring background actress Ivy Borg, seen as herself with no designed alien face.

basis.quote: As part of the 2011 Official Star Trek Convention in Las Vegas, Nevada, Hertzler appeared on stage with Gowron actor Robert O'Reilly, both in full Klingon makeup.
basis.establishes: The actor who played Martok, J.G. Hertzler, wore full Klingon makeup for the role.

basis.quote: I made teeth for him and tried to do something a little different with them.
basis.establishes: Makeup Designer Michael Westmore built custom facial appliances (teeth) for Martok, confirming heavy prosthetic makeup.

basis.quote: Because Hertzler is actually an artist too, appearing as Ritterhouse was easy for him, even though he had never before appeared on Star Trek without wearing makeup.
basis.establishes: J.G. Hertzler appeared as Ritterhouse bare-faced, without any makeup, unlike his other Star Trek roles.

basis.quote: The brief clips from Shane that appeared in this episode featured shots of Jack Palance as Jack Wilson.
basis.establishes: The character is archival film footage of actor Jack Palance appearing as himself, with no designed face.

basis.quote: Wilson was played by Jack Palance.
basis.establishes: The role is a Human film-clip character played by Jack Palance with his own face.

basis.quote: James Cromwell had difficulties filming in the heavy prosthetics required for the role of Hanok.
basis.establishes: The role required heavy prosthetics, hiding the actor under a designed face.

basis.quote: The makeup was pretty dominating.
basis.establishes: Actor James Cromwell describes the extensive, dominating makeup he wore for the role.

basis.quote: He had gone right from being Jimmy to being Vic - without a beat.
basis.establishes: Actor/singer James Darren slipped from himself directly into the Human lounge-singer role with no transformation, appearing essentially as himself.

basis.quote: Vic Fontaine was played by actor/singer James Darren.
basis.establishes: The Human singer hologram is played by a real singer appearing with his own face.

basis.quote: But the Ferengi makeup was much more uncomfortable.
basis.establishes: Jeffrey Combs wore full Ferengi head prosthetic makeup to play Brunt, describing the appliance as a 'big rubber thing.'

basis.quote: The Vorta makeup took approximately two and a half hours to be applied.
basis.establishes: The Vorta (Weyoun) look required a lengthy full facial/ear prosthetic makeup application.

basis.quote: Much of the workload which Makeup Supervisor Michael Westmore had to contend with during the seventh and final season of DS9 consisted of manufacturing Vorta ears for the character of Weyoun (along with Klingon foreheads for Worf).
basis.establishes: Weyoun specifically required manufactured prosthetic Vorta ears.

basis.establishes: The Vorta (Weyoun) look required a lengthy full facial/ear prosthetic makeup application.

basis.establishes: Weyoun specifically required manufactured prosthetic Vorta ears.

basis.establishes: Weyoun specifically required manufactured prosthetic Vorta ears.

basis.establishes: Weyoun specifically required manufactured prosthetic Vorta ears.

basis.quote: Visitor was relieved by the news that the Bajoran makeup was one of the most minor in the series.
basis.establishes: Bajoran makeup is minimal (a small nose appliance), so the performer's own face is seen.

basis.quote: Because each of the actors cast as Bajorans had to have their nosepiece applied each day, the DS9 makeup department had to have enough makeup artists to put on all the prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: The Bajoran appliance is a nosepiece only, leaving the actor visible as themselves.

basis.quote: The full-facial prosthetic masks used for the Jem'Hadar involved a lot of latex.
basis.establishes: Jem'Hadar performers wore full-facial latex prosthetic masks, hiding the actor.

basis.quote: Wearing the masks was considerably difficult for the required actors.
basis.establishes: The Jem'Hadar look was a full mask worn by the actors.

basis.quote: Physiologically, Farians had a quite similar external appearance to Humans except for a rising ridge that went from their nose to the top of their forehead.
basis.establishes: Farians look near-human with only a nose-to-forehead ridge, so the performer is largely seen as themselves.

basis.quote: After arriving at the Star Trek studios, he immediately went to have Kor's Klingon makeup applied for the first time.
basis.establishes: John Colicos wore designed Klingon makeup to play Kor.

basis.quote: The revised makeup involved in playing the part in "Blood Oath" regularly required Colicos to get up at 4 am.
basis.establishes: Kor's DS9-era Klingon makeup was extensive, requiring a very early call time.

basis.quote: Applying the Romulan prosthetics for Nemesis regularly took four hours.
basis.establishes: Romulans (Koval's species) wear a forehead prosthetic appliance; however Romulan makeup is a partial forehead ridge plus ears and the actor's core face remains visible, so whether the performer truly 'vanishes' is borderline. Koval's own page carries no makeup note, so this is flagged review.

basis.quote: Leopard-like spots on the side of his head identify him as a Trill.
basis.establishes: The only designed element is spots on the side of the head; the performer (John Glover) is otherwise seen as himself.

basis.quote: By DS9 Season 4, the duration needed to apply the Trill makeup for Jadzia Dax had been reduced to forty minutes daily, despite the Trill spots still being done entirely by hand.
basis.establishes: DS9 Trill makeup consists of hand-painted spots (light makeup), not a face-concealing appliance.

basis.quote: Runepp was played by John Paul Lona, a fan who won a guest spot on DS9 for designing the make-up of the Rasiinian species.
basis.establishes: The performer wore a purpose-designed alien-species makeup (won via a Playmates 'Design-an-Alien' contest), i.e. a designed face rather than his own appearance.

basis.quote: The makeup featured not only shoulders and the forehead piece but also a chin piece and a nose appliance.
basis.establishes: Cardassian characters (Rusot's species) wear extensive prosthetic appliances covering forehead, chin and nose.

basis.quote: When Cardassians began to appear in Star Trek, the makeup for the species took three-and-a-half hours to apply.
basis.establishes: The multi-hour prosthetic makeup confirms a full designed face that hides the performer.

basis.quote: The computer voice on most Federation starship and fixed installation computers was portrayed by Majel Barrett-Roddenberry since the early days of TOS.
basis.establishes: The computer voice is an unseen voice-only performance (never embodied on screen); DS9's computer voice was likewise provided by Judi Durand per the same page's performer list.

basis.quote: Having previously spent tiresome long durations required for makeup to be applied to him in other roles, Ken Marshall was extremely glad that, as Eddington, he was playing a Human; the only makeup appliances involved in the part were trademark Starfleet pointy sideburns.
basis.establishes: Eddington is Human and the actor wore no concealing makeup — only pointed sideburns — so the audience sees the performer as himself.

basis.quote: The executives insisted the make-up be changed via removal of the forehead markings; having spent a long time seeking a beautiful actress for the role of Dax, Paramount wanted to avoid Farrell being defaced by prosthetics.
basis.establishes: DS9 Trills deliberately have no concealing forehead prosthetics — only hand-painted spots — so the performer (Kevin Rahm as this Trill) is seen as himself.

basis.quote: By DS9 Season 4, the duration needed to apply the Trill makeup for Jadzia Dax had been reduced to forty minutes daily, despite the Trill spots still being done entirely by hand.
basis.establishes: DS9 Trill makeup is light hand-painted spots, not a face-concealing appliance.

basis.quote: Swink had earlier played the Bajoran character Rozahn, but she found the Vorta makeup more difficult to endure.
basis.establishes: The Vorta makeup worn by Kitty Swink as Luaran was an extensive, difficult prosthetic makeup.

basis.quote: the contact lenses drove me nuts! And it is a lot more appliance than the Bajoran nose.
basis.establishes: The Vorta look required violet contact lenses and substantial prosthetic appliance, hiding the performer's face.

basis.quote: Vorta had pale skin, violet eyes, and elongated ears that were completely joined to the rest of the head.
basis.establishes: The Vorta species has a designed non-human face (violet eyes, elongated fused ears) requiring contact lenses and ear prosthetics.

basis.quote: the contact lenses drove me nuts! And it is a lot more appliance than the Bajoran nose.
basis.establishes: Vorta makeup (the species Gelnon belongs to) consists of violet contact lenses plus a substantial prosthetic appliance.

basis.quote: Kira Meru was a female Bajoran refugee during the Occupation of Bajor in the 24th century.
basis.establishes: The character is Bajoran.

basis.quote: Externally, Bajorans resembled Humans in appearance. They were distinguished by a series of four to seven horizontal creases across their noses.
basis.establishes: Bajorans wear only a small nasal appliance and otherwise look Human, so the performer's face is seen as themselves.

basis.quote: Siana was a female Cardassian who lived on Cardassia Prime in the late-24th century.
basis.establishes: The character is Cardassian.

basis.quote: Externally, Cardassians were easily recognizable by each having light-gray skin, two thick, vertical neck ridges that receded back to the crown of their head, and an inverted tear-shaped ridge in the center of the forehead.
basis.establishes: Cardassians have a heavily designed face and head (gray skin, neck ridges, forehead ridge) built from prosthetics.

basis.quote: The Cardassians thus became the first Star Trek species for which the makeup team created a shoulder piece.
basis.establishes: Cardassian makeup was so extensive it required a custom prosthetic shoulder piece.

basis.quote: Winn was played by actress Louise Fletcher.
basis.establishes: The recognizable actress Louise Fletcher played the role.

basis.quote: Externally, Bajorans resembled Humans in appearance. They were distinguished by a series of four to seven horizontal creases across their noses.
basis.establishes: As a Bajoran, Winn wore only a small nasal appliance and was otherwise Human-looking, so Fletcher is seen as herself.

basis.quote: Lwaxana Troi was a Betazoid Federation ambassador, and the mother of Deanna Troi.
basis.establishes: The character is Betazoid.

basis.quote: Externally, Betazoids were physically indistinguishable from Humans in every aspect but one: the irises of their eyes were completely black.
basis.establishes: Betazoids look identical to Humans (only black contact lenses), so Majel Barrett is seen as herself.

basis.quote: This character was only mentioned in dialogue.
basis.establishes: Anjohl Tennan never appeared on screen, so no performer appears in makeup as this character.

basis.quote: Burt Ryan was a plain clothes officer of the New York City Police Department during the 1950s.
basis.establishes: A Human 1950s NYPD officer role, worn without any alien makeup.

basis.quote: He held a likeness to Gul Dukat.
basis.establishes: The audience sees Marc Alaimo's own bare face here (resembling, but unmasked by, his Cardassian Dukat role).

basis.quote: To prepare for assuming the role of Dukat, Marc Alaimo routinely had to endure several hours in which makeup was applied to him and he was fitted into his Cardassian costume.
basis.establishes: Playing Dukat required several hours of Cardassian prosthetic makeup application each time.

basis.quote: Wearing the Dukat makeup was no problem for Alaimo, as he had many years' experience with such makeup for stage productions.
basis.establishes: Alaimo wore extensive Dukat makeup that transformed his face.

basis.quote: Carl Zeemo was a holographic simulation of a Human mob godfather character in the Bashir 62 holoprogram.
basis.establishes: A Human role played without alien makeup.

basis.quote: We wanted an actor who would carry the weight of history to any film buffs who were watching the show.
basis.establishes: The production cast recognizable film-noir actor Marc Lawrence for his own recognizable face, seen as himself.

basis.quote: However, Jeri Taylor convinced them that the Klingon makeup would hide this fact, making Sloyan less recognizable.
basis.establishes: The Klingon makeup conceals/hides the actor's identity — the audience does not see the performer as themselves.

basis.quote: Add to that the fact that Klingon actors need to go through three hours of makeup in the morning, and suddenly you have very little time with your actor.
basis.establishes: Klingon makeup for this character is a heavy, hours-long prosthetic application.

basis.quote: In the episode Who Mourns for Morn?, Shepherd, without the Morn makeup, briefly appeared as the Bajoran customer whom Quark asks to fill Morn's seat.
basis.establishes: Without the Morn makeup the same actor is unrecognizable, appearing as an entirely different (Bajoran) character — the Morn makeup fully hides the performer.

basis.quote: This scene remained through a number of script rewrites, to the point that Shepherd (in full Morn makeup) was actually fitted for a tuxedo, but at the last minute, the scene was removed.
basis.establishes: The actor performs the role in 'full Morn makeup' (a full-head Lurian prosthetic).

basis.quote: At the start of DS9, the duration required for applying the Ferengi makeup was approximately three hours.
basis.establishes: Lonzo is a Ferengi; the Ferengi look is a heavy, three-hour prosthetic makeup.

basis.quote: It consisted of two prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: The Ferengi makeup is a multi-piece prosthetic headpiece/face appliance that transforms the actor.

basis.quote: The full-facial prosthetic masks used for the Jem'Hadar involved a lot of latex.
basis.establishes: Temo'Zuma is a Jem'Hadar; Jem'Hadar are portrayed with full-facial latex prosthetic masks that hide the performer.

basis.quote: Physically distinguished by upswept eyebrows and pointed ears.
basis.establishes: Chu'lak is a Vulcan; Vulcans are distinguished only by eyebrows and ear appliances (light makeup), leaving the actor's face visible as himself.

basis.quote: Visitor was relieved by the news that the Bajoran makeup was one of the most minor in the series.
basis.establishes: Mika is a Bajoran; Bajoran makeup is among the most minor in the franchise (a small nose appliance), so the actress is seen as herself.

basis.quote: Because each of the actors cast as Bajorans had to have their nosepiece applied each day, the DS9 makeup department had to have enough makeup artists to put on all the prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: The only Bajoran appliance is a nosepiece — a light appliance, not a designed face.

basis.quote: At the start of DS9, the duration required for applying the Ferengi makeup was approximately three hours.
basis.establishes: Gint is a Ferengi (played by Max Grodenchik in the Ferengi look); the Ferengi makeup is a heavy three-hour prosthetic.

basis.quote: It consisted of two prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: The Ferengi appearance is a multi-piece prosthetic headpiece that transforms the actor.

basis.quote: At the start of DS9, the duration required for applying the Ferengi makeup was approximately three hours.
basis.establishes: Rom is a Ferengi; the Ferengi look is a heavy three-hour prosthetic makeup.

basis.quote: It consisted of two prosthetic appliances.
basis.establishes: The Ferengi makeup is a multi-piece prosthetic headpiece and face appliance that hides the performer's own features.

basis.quote: They almost always wore refrigeration suits that entirely concealed their bodies.
basis.establishes: Pran is a Breen; Breen are fully concealed in refrigeration suits, so the performer is entirely hidden.

basis.quote: According to Worf, no outsider had ever seen what a Breen looked like under their refrigeration suits and lived.
basis.establishes: The Breen are never seen out of their masked, helmeted suits — the actor is completely unseen.

basis.quote: Her features are vaguely feline, though you might have to look twice to see she wasn't Human.
basis.establishes: Onaya's humanoid form is nearly indistinguishable from Human — only minimal makeup, so actress Meg Foster is seen essentially as herself.

basis.quote: a tall humanoid alien, attractive yet capable-looking.
basis.establishes: Mareel is described as an attractive humanoid alien with no designed/creature face — actress Megan Gallagher is seen as herself (Tyrans bear only light forehead ridges).

basis.quote: In the episode's script, Caprice is described as "a gorgeous woman... She's wearing a dress slit way too high on the side and a neckline that plunges way too low."
basis.establishes: Caprice is a Human holographic character described as an ordinary (glamorous) woman — the actress appears as herself, no prosthetics.

basis.quote: Duchamps temporarily took on the physical characteristics of Worf when his transporter pattern was stored in the holodeck following the explosion of the USS Orinoco.
basis.establishes: This holographic character is seen wearing Worf's physical form, i.e. Michael Dorn as the Klingon Worf.

basis.quote: While the prosthetics initially took two and a half hours to apply each time, by the end of TNG the process was shortened to about two hours.
basis.establishes: Michael Dorn's Worf appearance (which Duchamps takes on) is a full Klingon prosthetic makeup.

basis.quote: In fact, the role of Hawkins was Dorn's only appearance without prosthetic makeup.
basis.establishes: Michael Dorn played this role bare-faced, with no prosthetic makeup, so the audience sees the performer as himself.

basis.quote: I just loved not being in makeup.
basis.establishes: Dorn confirms he was out of his usual Klingon makeup for this role.

basis.quote: While the prosthetics initially took two and a half hours to apply each time, by the end of TNG the process was shortened to about two hours.
basis.establishes: Worf is realized through hours-long full Klingon prosthetic makeup on Michael Dorn.

basis.quote: Worf's ridge patterns evolved due to experimentation by Michael Westmore, mainly for aesthetics, but also to be more comfortable for the actor.
basis.establishes: The Klingon cranial ridges are a designed prosthetic appliance created by the makeup department.

basis.quote: While the prosthetics initially took two and a half hours to apply each time, by the end of TNG the process was shortened to about two hours.
basis.establishes: Mirror Worf is the Klingon counterpart played by the same actor (Michael Dorn) under the same full Klingon prosthetic makeup.

basis.quote: I ended up sculpting a little nose as something we could possibly use and that's what they went with.
basis.establishes: Bajoran makeup (Saghi's species) is only a small nose appliance, so the performer is mostly seen as themselves.

basis.quote: Considering how he could give the Bajorans a subtle alien appearance, Michael Westmore thought about adding a prosthetic make-up appliance to either the forehead, the end of the nose, or the cheeks.
basis.establishes: The Bajoran design was deliberately subtle, a single small appliance rather than a face-covering prosthetic.

basis.quote: The appliance used for the nose and forehead downplayed the alien nature of the Trills, causing them to seem just slightly extraterrestrial but still overtly Human.
basis.establishes: Trill makeup (Janel's species) is minimal and leaves the performer looking overtly Human, i.e. seen as themselves.

basis.quote: Most Trills were distinguished by two rows of typically dark spots going down each side of their bodies, from the top of their forehead down their neck to their toes.
basis.establishes: The Trill design is essentially painted-on spots, a light-makeup marking rather than a designed face that hides the performer.

basis.quote: As the holographic character of Komananov assumes the physical characteristics of Kira Nerys in "Our Man Bashir", the role was played by Kira actress Nana Visitor.
basis.establishes: The character is seen as Kira Nerys played by Nana Visitor, i.e. the performer as herself in only the minimal Bajoran makeup.

basis.quote: Visitor was relieved by the news that the Bajoran makeup was one of the most minor in the series.
basis.establishes: The Bajoran (Kira) makeup that Komananov appears in is one of the most minor appliances in the franchise, so the performer is seen as herself.

basis.quote: Kay Eaton was played by Nana Visitor, who usually portrayed Kira Nerys.
basis.establishes: This is a 1950s Human writer role played by Nana Visitor out of her usual Bajoran makeup.

basis.quote: I fashioned my look on a self-portrait my mother had done of herself when she was my age at that time period
basis.establishes: Visitor styled her own bare-faced human appearance after her mother's portrait, confirming she appears as herself.

basis.quote: Visitor was relieved by the news that the Bajoran makeup was one of the most minor in the series.
basis.establishes: Kira's Bajoran makeup (Nana Visitor) is one of the most minor appliances in the series, so the audience sees the performer as herself.

basis.quote: It's just a small elephant nose that you wear.
basis.establishes: The entire Bajoran/Kira appliance is a small nose piece, a light makeup rather than a designed face that hides the performer.

basis.quote: Lela Dax in the body of Kira Nerys was played by Nana Visitor during Jadzia Dax's zhian'tara in the episode "Facets".
basis.establishes: The role was performed by Nana Visitor as her recurring Bajoran character Kira Nerys (a light nasal-appliance role in which the performer's face is fully seen), not under a designed/hidden face.

basis.quote: Lola was played by actress Nana Visitor, who did her own singing in character.
basis.establishes: A Human holodeck lounge singer performed and sung by Nana Visitor herself, seen and heard as the performer (no designed/hidden face).

basis.quote: Luma Rahl was an alias assumed by Kira Nerys when she traveled back in time from 2374 to 2346, using the Orb of Time, to investigate Dukat's claim that he and her mother, Kira Meru, were lovers.
basis.establishes: The character is simply Kira Nerys (Bajoran, light nasal appliance) using a false name, i.e. the performer Nana Visitor seen as her ordinary self.

basis.quote: Commander Kolana was a female Klingon warrior.
basis.establishes: The character is a Klingon, a species portrayed with full forehead-ridge prosthetic appliances, hiding the performer under a designed face.

basis.quote: Kolana was played by actress Nancy Youngblut.
basis.establishes: Identifies the performer beneath the Klingon prosthetics.

basis.quote: Darok was a Klingon who served aboard the IKS Ch'Tang in 2375.
basis.establishes: The character is a Klingon, portrayed with full forehead-ridge prosthetic appliances, hiding the performer under a designed face.

basis.quote: Darok was played by Neil Vipond.
basis.establishes: Identifies the performer beneath the Klingon prosthetics.

basis.quote: Liam Bilby was a Human operative in the Orion Syndicate in the 2370s.
basis.establishes: The character is Human, requiring no species prosthetics.

basis.quote: Liam Bilby was played by Nick Tate.
basis.establishes: Identifies the performer, who appears as himself in this Human role.

basis.quote: Ezri Dax was a joined Trill consisting of the Dax symbiont and the ninth host of the symbiont, formerly known as Ezri Tigan.
basis.establishes: The character is a Trill, a species portrayed with only light spot makeup along a fully visible face.

basis.quote: Ezri Dax was played by Nicole de Boer.
basis.establishes: Identifies the performer, whose own face is seen (only Trill spot makeup applied).

basis.quote: Ezri Tigan was an unjoined Trill mercenary who along with Brunt frequently worked for the Klingon-Cardassian Alliance.
basis.establishes: The mirror-universe character is a Trill, a species portrayed with only light spot makeup on a fully visible face.

basis.quote: Ezri Tigan was played by Nicole de Boer.
basis.establishes: Identifies the performer, whose own face is seen (only Trill spot makeup).

basis.quote: Vedek Fala was a male Bajoran religious teacher in the 24th century Bajoran Republic.
basis.establishes: The character is a Bajoran, a species portrayed with only a light nasal-ridge appliance and an otherwise fully visible face.

basis.quote: Fala was played by actor Norman Parker.
basis.establishes: Identifies the performer, seen as himself apart from the light Bajoran nose appliance.

basis.quote: Tagana was a Bajoran nurse who was assigned to Deep Space 9.
basis.establishes: The character is a Bajoran, a species portrayed with only a light nasal-ridge appliance and an otherwise fully visible face.

basis.quote: Nurse Tagana was played by stunt actress Patricia Tallman who received no credit for her appearance.
basis.establishes: Identifies the performer, seen as herself apart from the light Bajoran nose appliance.

basis.quote: Captain Tim Watters was a male Human Starfleet cadet in the 24th century.
basis.establishes: The character is Human, requiring no species prosthetics.

basis.quote: Watters was played by Paul Popowich.
basis.establishes: Identifies the performer, who appears as himself in this Human role.

basis.quote: Cassie was played by Penny Johnson.
basis.establishes: Identifies the performer of this 1950s Human waitress, who appears as herself.

basis.establishes: Character is Human, so the actress appears as an ordinary human woman

basis.quote: Kasidy Yates was portrayed by Penny Johnson.
basis.establishes: Human actress plays a Human character, seen as herself with no designed face

basis.quote: Melanie was a young woman from the mid-25th century in an alternate timeline who aspired to become a writer.

basis.quote: Pabst was played by actor René Auberjonois.
basis.establishes: Auberjonois plays a 1950s Human editor character

basis.quote: Although Auberjonois usually played Odo on Star Trek: Deep Space Nine, the actor had no difficulty with separating Pabst from Odo in his performance choices.
basis.establishes: Auberjonois appears as a bare-faced Human here, distinct from his Odo makeup role

basis.quote: Auberjonois was not allowed to eat or chew gum while filming, for fear it would ruin the make-up, although Salome Jens managed to get away with chewing gum on the set.
basis.establishes: Auberjonois wore facial make-up (the Changeling's smooth designed face) so restrictive he could not eat or chew

basis.quote: Considering how he could give the Bajorans a subtle alien appearance, Michael Westmore thought about adding a prosthetic make-up appliance to either the forehead, the end of the nose, or the cheeks.
basis.establishes: Porta is a Bajoran; Bajoran makeup is a single small nose appliance, so the performer's face is otherwise their own and seen as themselves.

basis.quote: Part of the reason the Bajoran makeup's so... spare,
basis.establishes: The Bajoran makeup is deliberately minimal/spare, confirming the actor is largely seen as themselves.

basis.quote: Markalians were characterized by a hairless head with small scales and thorns all over their faces, a striped pattern on the backs of their skulls, and small, oval depressions on the sides of their heads.
basis.establishes: Regana Tosh is a Markalian; the whole head and face are covered in a sculpted scaled/thorned prosthetic appearance.

basis.quote: The alien's makeup was designed by Michael Westmore, under advisement to keep in mind that the same alien "may come back."
basis.establishes: The Markalian appearance (reused for Regana Tosh) is a full Westmore-designed makeup, a designed face.

basis.quote: The most distinctive feature of the modern Klingon was a sagittal crest, beginning on the forehead and often continuing over the skull.
basis.establishes: Ch'Pok is a Klingon; the species wears a sculpted forehead/skull prosthetic covering the head, a designed face.

basis.quote: Considering how he could give the Bajorans a subtle alien appearance, Michael Westmore thought about adding a prosthetic make-up appliance to either the forehead, the end of the nose, or the cheeks.
basis.establishes: Razka Karn is a Bajoran; Bajoran makeup is a single small nose appliance, so the performer's face is otherwise their own and seen as themselves.

basis.quote: Part of the reason the Bajoran makeup's so... spare,

basis.quote: For each of the Founders with dialogue, a mold was taken of their face then an Odo-like mask was sculpted over it.

basis.quote: Consequently, the makeup department ultimately had to craft new masks for principal Changelings.

basis.quote: The most distinctive feature of the modern Klingon was a sagittal crest, beginning on the forehead and often continuing over the skull.
basis.establishes: Ch'Targh is a Klingon; the species wears a sculpted forehead/skull prosthetic covering the head, a designed face.

basis.quote: The most distinctive feature of the modern Klingon was a sagittal crest, beginning on the forehead and often continuing over the skull.
basis.establishes: Chu'vok is a Klingon general; the species wears a sculpted forehead/skull prosthetic covering the head, a designed face.

basis.quote: The ring of small horns encircling the Jem'Hadar face and on the crown of the head likewise had multiple sources of inspiration.
basis.establishes: Virak'kara is a Jem'Hadar; the face and head are covered in a sculpted horned prosthetic makeup, a designed face.

basis.quote: Before designing the look of the Jem'Hadar, Makeup Supervisor Michael Westmore was told to "design something that was tough, that they could shoot at but they couldn't hurt, they were indestructible, as an army they were unstoppable, and they would have thick skin."
basis.establishes: The Jem'Hadar were a fully Westmore-designed makeup/creature look with thick reptilian hide.

basis.quote: Goran'Agar was a Jem'Hadar First, whose ship crash landed on the planet Bopak III in 2369.
basis.establishes: The character is a Jem'Hadar, a species rendered in full prosthetic makeup.

basis.quote: Therefore, DS9's makeup department sought to give the Jem'Hadar a look that portrayed toughness and resiliency.
basis.establishes: The Jem'Hadar appearance was a full designed makeup look created by the makeup department.

basis.quote: Kudak'Etan was a Jem'Hadar soldier bred for combat during the Dominion War.
basis.establishes: The character is a Jem'Hadar, a full-prosthetic species.

basis.establishes: The performer wore a costume with rubber accoutrements as part of the Jem'Hadar makeup/costume.

basis.quote: Therefore, DS9's makeup department sought to give the Jem'Hadar a look that portrayed toughness and resiliency.
basis.establishes: The Jem'Hadar were a full designed makeup look.

basis.quote: His character's exact ancestry is never specified on screen, except by inferences viewers can make by the character's name.

basis.establishes: The Klingon face the audience sees is a sculpted forehead/skull crest (designed face).

basis.quote: Michael Westmore designed Klingon feet and a Klingon spine.
basis.establishes: Klingon prosthetic anatomy was built by the makeup department.

basis.quote: Rear Admiral Rollman was a Human female Starfleet flag officer who served during the mid-24th century.
basis.establishes: The character is Human, seen as the performer herself.

basis.quote: Admiral Rollman was played by actress Susan Bay who is the widow of Spock actor, Leonard Nimoy.
basis.establishes: A bare-faced Human role played by the actress.

basis.quote: Benteen was played by actress Susan Gibney, who also played TNG character Leah Brahms.
basis.establishes: A Human Starfleet officer played bare-faced by the actress (the same actress recognizable as another human TNG character).

basis.quote: Lenara Kahn was a female Trill who served as a scientist at the Trill Science Ministry during the late-24th century.
basis.establishes: The character is a Trill (DS9 spotted variety).

basis.quote: He applied the spots by hand, employing a technique whereby he deliberately made his hands quiver while painting the markings on.
basis.establishes: DS9 Trill makeup is only hand-painted spots, not a face-obscuring appliance.

basis.quote: It's not much different than freckles or leopard spots or zebra stripes," Farrell observed.
basis.establishes: The spots are a minor, natural-looking marking, so the performer is seen as herself.

basis.quote: Uri'lash was the Hupyrian servant to Brunt during his brief stint as (acting) Grand Nagus.
basis.establishes: The character is a Hupyrian.

basis.quote: Hupyrians were taller than most humanoid species, and characterized by parallel skin creases around their eyes, mouth, and ears.
basis.establishes: Hupyrians have a non-human designed facial appearance (skin creases around eyes/mouth/ears); note this is an in-universe biology description, not an explicit makeup/background note, so no makeup sentence was found affirming the prosthetic work.

basis.quote: Aluura was a Human dabo girl who began working at Quark's Bar, Grill, Gaming House and Holosuite Arcade in 2374.
basis.establishes: The character is Human, seen as the performer herself.

basis.quote: They were distinguished by a series of four to seven horizontal creases across their noses.
basis.establishes: Kesha is Bajoran; the species' only alien feature is light nasal-ridge creases, so the performer (Tami-Adrian George) is seen bare-faced as herself. Kesha's own page carries no makeup note.

basis.quote: Darlene Kursky was Douglas Pabst's new secretary at Incredible Tales in 1953.
basis.establishes: An ordinary 1953 Human secretary (species Human) played by Terry Farrell with no alien/designed face; the performer appears as herself.

basis.quote: In 2372 the character of Bare temporarily assumed the physical characteristics of Jadzia Dax after her transporter pattern was stored in the holodeck following the explosion of the USS Orinoco.
basis.establishes: A Human hologram played by Terry Farrell who on screen wears Jadzia Dax's (Terry Farrell's) own face; the audience sees the performer as herself, no designed face.

basis.quote: This makeup was used on all Trill afterward.
basis.establishes: After rejecting a forehead appliance, the production settled on giving Jadzia only spots; the reduced spot makeup leaves Terry Farrell's face fully visible as herself.

basis.quote: Most Trills were distinguished by two rows of typically dark spots going down each side of their bodies, from the top of their forehead down their neck to their toes.
basis.establishes: Trill appearance is achieved with airbrushed spots (light makeup), not a designed face; the performer is seen as herself.

basis.quote: They were distinguished by a series of four to seven horizontal creases across their noses.
basis.establishes: Kira Taban is Bajoran; the species' sole alien feature is light nasal-ridge creases, so the performer (Thomas Kopache) is seen bare-faced as himself. His own page carries no makeup note.

basis.quote: They were distinguished by a series of four to seven horizontal creases across their noses.
basis.establishes: Heler is Bajoran; the species' only alien feature is a light nasal-ridge appliance, so the performer (Thomas Prisco) is seen as himself. His own page carries no makeup note.

basis.quote: Jack was a genetically-engineered Human born during the 24th century.
basis.establishes: An ordinary-looking Human (Augment) played bare-faced by Tim Ransom with no designed alien face; the performer is seen as himself.

basis.quote: The most distinctive feature of the modern Klingon was a sagittal crest, beginning on the forehead and often continuing over the skull.
basis.establishes: T'Kar is a Klingon; the species' defining look is a sculpted cranial/forehead crest, a heavy prosthetic designed face that Tim Russ vanishes under. The character page has no makeup note, so this species descriptor is the affirmative basis.

basis.quote: Most Trills were distinguished by two rows of typically dark spots going down each side of their bodies, from the top of their forehead down their neck to their toes.
basis.establishes: Bejal is Trill; Trill appearance is achieved with airbrushed spots (light makeup), not a designed face, so the performer (Tim Ryan) is seen as himself. His own page carries no makeup note.

basis.quote: According to makeup supervisor, Maihar'du's makeup was based upon that of Pruneface from Dick Tracy.
basis.establishes: Maihar'du (Hupyrian, Tiny Ron) wears a heavy full-face prosthetic makeup modeled on Dick Tracy's Pruneface, a designed face the performer vanishes under.

basis.quote: They almost always wore refrigeration suits that entirely concealed their bodies.
basis.establishes: Thot Gor is Breen; Breen are entirely concealed in helmeted refrigeration suits, so the performer (Todd Slayton) is never seen — face and body fully hidden under a designed costume.

basis.quote: An additional motive for Behr deciding that the Breen should appear in fully enclosed costumes was that this would cut down on make-up expenses.
basis.establishes: Production note confirming Breen appear in fully enclosed costumes, hiding the performer completely.

basis.quote: The makeup featured not only shoulders and the forehead piece but also a chin piece and a nose appliance.
basis.establishes: Lasaran is Cardassian; the species is realized with extensive multi-piece prosthetic makeup (forehead, chin, nose, shoulders, neck ridges), a designed face the performer (Todd Waring) vanishes under. His own page has no makeup note.

basis.quote: Janklow was an enlisted Starfleet engineer assigned to the USS Defiant in 2372.
basis.establishes: An ordinary Human Starfleet engineer (species Human) played bare-faced by stunt actor Tom Morga; no designed alien face, performer seen as himself.

basis.quote: His make-up is that of a Markalian, which is why he is identified as such here.
basis.establishes: Madrat wears full Markalian alien prosthetic makeup, a designed face the performer (stunt actor Tom Morga) vanishes under.

basis.quote: Soto was played by regular stunt actor Tom Morga.
basis.establishes: Soto is a Lethean played by stunt performer Tom Morga.

basis.quote: This was despite the fact that the Lethean involved recycled makeup.
basis.establishes: The Lethean species is realized through a full makeup/prosthetic build.

basis.quote: so the headpiece is converted from another alien.
basis.establishes: The Lethean look is a full prosthetic headpiece appliance, hiding the performer's face.

basis.quote: Like Kurn, Rodek was portrayed by Tony Todd.
basis.establishes: Rodek is a Klingon portrayed by Tony Todd.

basis.quote: For scenes from "Ethics" featuring actor Michael Dorn as Worf, Michael Westmore designed Klingon feet and a Klingon spine.
basis.establishes: The Klingon appearance is a Michael Westmore prosthetic makeup design, i.e. a full designed alien face/body.

basis.quote: Danar was played by veteran Star Trek actor Vaughn Armstrong in his second of many appearances.
basis.establishes: Danar is a Cardassian played by Vaughn Armstrong.

basis.quote: One of the nice things is that you get to put on all that makeup, and suddenly you have a Cardassian neck, for instance, that looks like it's been straining with tension for fifty million years.
basis.establishes: The Cardassian role required extensive prosthetic makeup that transforms the performer's face and neck.

basis.quote: Zek was played by Wallace Shawn.
basis.establishes: Zek is a Ferengi played by Wallace Shawn.

basis.quote: But to be trapped inside that head for at least 12 hours, not counting three hours to put the makeup on and an hour to take it off, was a little bit disturbing and uncomfortable and exhausting.
basis.establishes: The Ferengi Zek required a full prosthetic head and hours of makeup, hiding the performer's own face.
